steps.action: Syrtos is a traditional Greek dance in which the with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.
steps.text: Syrtos is a traditional Greek dance in which the with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: Syrtos and its relative kalamatianos are the mos
steps.text: Syrtos and its relative kalamatianos are the most popular dances throughout Greece and Cyprus, and are frequently danced by the Greek diaspora worldwide.

steps.action: Syrtos and kalamatianos use the same dance steps
steps.text: Syrtos and kalamatianos use the same dance steps, but the syrtos is in 44 time and the kalamatianos is in 78 time, organized in a slow (3 beat), quick (2 beat), quick (2 beat) rhythm.

steps.action: Syrtos and kalamatianos are line dances and circ
steps.text: Syrtos and kalamatianos are line dances and circle dances, done with the dancers in a curving line holding hands, facing right.

steps.action: The dancer at the right end of the line is the l
steps.text: The dancer at the right end of the line is the leader.

steps.action: He may also be a solo performer, improvising sho
steps.text: He may also be a solo performer, improvising showy twisting skillful moves as the rest of the line does the basic step.

steps.action: While he does this, the next dancer in line stop
steps.text: While he does this, the next dancer in line stops dancing and holds him up with a twisted handkerchief linking their hands, so he can turn and not fall down, as in the Antikristos.

steps.action: In some parts of syrtos, pairs of dancers hold a
steps.text: In some parts of syrtos, pairs of dancers hold a handkerchief from its two sides.

steps.action: Rennell Rodd (1892) suggests that the dance is a
steps.text: Rennell Rodd (1892) suggests that the dance is an imitation of the action of drawing in the seine net.

steps.action: Dimaras describes an inscription from the times
steps.text: Dimaras describes an inscription from the times of Caligula, which implied that already at these times Syrtos was considered an ancient Greek dance of local tradition.

steps.action: The steps of the Kalamatianos are the same as th
steps.text: The steps of the Kalamatianos are the same as those of the Syrtos, but the latter is slower and more stately, its beat being an even 44.

steps.action: Traditionally, it was danced by segregated lines
steps.text: Traditionally, it was danced by segregated lines of men and women.

steps.action: The lead dancer usually holds the second dancer
steps.text: The lead dancer usually holds the second dancer by a handkerchief.

steps.action: The name of the dance comes from the cut in tune
steps.text: The name of the dance comes from the cut in tune/music.

steps.action: It is a faster syrto sta dyo style dance.
steps.text: It is a faster syrto sta dyo style dance.

steps.action: When the music stops the dancers yell "Hey".
steps.text: When the music stops the dancers yell "Hey".

steps.action: When the music stops one also can put their arms
steps.text: When the music stops one also can put their arms up, down, or clap.

steps.action: It can also be danced going backwards and forwar
steps.text: It can also be danced going backwards and forwards or with partners.

steps.action: Thus, these two melodies, the archetypes of all
steps.text: Thus, these two melodies, the archetypes of all other Cretan Syrtoi, were introduced to Crete and in subsequent years they were put into dance.

steps.action: It has been observed by authority of Greek tradi
steps.text: It has been observed by authority of Greek traditional music Domna Samiou, that the Cretan Syrtos resembles to a certain degree the dances of the same name from Constantinople, the Aegean and Asia Minor.

steps.action: This older form of the dance features men and wo
steps.text: This older form of the dance features men and women in two separate lines, the men behind the women.

steps.action: They merge into one line of mixed men and women
steps.text: They merge into one line of mixed men and women and then back to the two lines, using the ancient chain hold that can be seen on ancient Greek vase paintings.

steps.action: Syrto (from the Greek word "syro" meaning to pul
steps.text: Syrto (from the Greek word "syro" meaning to pull or, more accurately, to lead) is characterized by its slow-quick-quick rhythm within its 44 meter.

steps.action: There is also a similar dance, the Silivrianos S
steps.text: There is also a similar dance, the Silivrianos Syrtos.
